title: Minimum spanning trees

publication.changeSummary: Language-neutral DSA draft with explicit C/C++/Java/Python mapping for Minimum spanning trees; independent algorithm, implementation, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Algorithmic Foundations; August 2023; material applicable to Minimum spanning trees
sources.claim: A minimum spanning tree connects all vertices of a weighted undirected graph with minimum total edge weight and no cycles.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A minimum spanning tree connects all vertices of a weighted undirected graph with minimum total edge weight and no cycles.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Kruskal sorts edges and adds one only when disjoint-set representatives differ. Your goal is to implement and test one MST algorithm and distinguish MST from shortest-path trees. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Graph representation and traversal; Priority queue applications; Unweighted and Dijkstra shortest paths; Make, find and union. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Shared concept and invariant
sections.body: A minimum spanning tree connects all vertices of a weighted undirected graph with minimum total edge weight and no cycles. This claim is assessed independently from syntax so conceptual evidence can transfer.

sections.heading: C, C++, Java, and Python implementation map
sections.body: In C, represent adjacency and disjoint sets with explicit buffers and ownership; C++ uses vector-based lists and standard priority structures; Java uses typed collections and careful object equality; Python uses list/dict/set and heapq, with the same graph or partition invariant and complexity target.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Disconnected graphs yield a forest, equal weights can yield many MSTs, and shortest paths are a different objective. The draft therefore rejects: A minimum spanning tree also gives the shortest path between every pair.

includes: Use Prim or Kruskal to connect an undirected weighted graph with minimum total edge cost.
Implement and test one MST algorithm and distinguish MST from shortest-path trees.

In C, represent adjacency and disjoint sets with explicit buffers and ownership; C++ uses vector-based lists and standard priority structures; Java uses typed collections and careful object equality; Python uses list/dict/set and heapq, with the same graph or partition invariant and complexity target.
excludes: Disconnected graphs yield a forest, equal weights can yield many MSTs, and shortest paths are a different objective.

outcomes: Implement and test one MST algorithm and distinguish MST from shortest-path trees.

examples.title: Minimum spanning trees: language-neutral trace
examples.situation: Kruskal sorts edges and adds one only when disjoint-set representatives differ.
walkthrough: Step 1 — State the abstract input and invariant: Kruskal sorts edges and adds one only when disjoint-set representatives differ.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: MST minimizes total tree weight, not each pairwise route; shortest-path and spanning-tree objectives differ.
examples.result: The conceptual result follows because MST minimizes total tree weight, not each pairwise route; shortest-path and spanning-tree objectives differ.

examples.title: Minimum spanning trees: implementation transfer
examples.situation: In C, represent adjacency and disjoint sets with explicit buffers and ownership; C++ uses vector-based lists and standard priority structures; Java uses typed collections and careful object equality; Python uses list/dict/set and heapq, with the same graph or partition invariant and complexity target.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Keep the same specification and invariant across all four languages.

Step 2 — Map storage, library, equality, bounds, ownership, and error rules using: In C, represent adjacency and disjoint sets with explicit buffers and ownership; C++ uses vector-based lists and standard priority structures; Java uses typed collections and careful object equality; Python uses list/dict/set and heapq, with the same graph or partition invariant and complexity target.
Step 3 — Retest this boundary in the selected implementation: Disconnected graphs yield a forest, equal weights can yield many MSTs, and shortest paths are a different objective.
examples.result: Concept mastery transfers, while implementation evidence remains language-specific.

misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Use an invariant or counterexample to explain why this DSA belief fails: A minimum spanning tree also gives the shortest path between every pair.

analogy.example: A carefully labeled physical arrangement can introduce Minimum spanning trees, while the canonical invariant and operations remain the actual algorithmic model.
usefulFor: Introducing structure, order, connectivity, or state transition before formal notation
limitations: A physical arrangement does not reproduce asymptotic cost, aliasing, numeric limits, recursion depth, or the four languages' implementation rules.
